'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the [[:en:Folk religion|folk beliefs]] of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter [[:en:Salt|salt]] or [[:en:Bean|beans]].{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Arai Hakuseki|Arai Hakuseki]]'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Yamagata Bantō|Yamakata Bantō]]'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]]'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

To keep them away, people scatter [[:en:Salt|salt]] or [[:en:Bean|beans]].{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Arai Hakuseki|Arai Hakuseki]]'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Yamagata Bantō|Yamakata Bantō]]'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]]'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5623686]] Wikidata name: Gwisin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=There is an enwiki article but it seems horribly incomplete and possibly completely wrong scope[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 17:58, 13 February 2025 (UTC)|qq=}}
*
'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the [[:en:Folk religion|folk beliefs]] of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter [[:en:Salt|salt]] or [[:en:Bean|beans]].{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Arai Hakuseki|Arai Hakuseki]]'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Yamagata Bantō|Yamakata Bantō]]'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]]'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5623686]] Wikidata name: Gwisin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=There is an enwiki article but it seems horribly incomplete and possibly completely wrong scope[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 17:58, 13 February 2025 (UTC)|qq=}}
*
'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the [[:en:Folk religion|folk beliefs]] of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter [[:en:Salt|salt]] or [[:en:Bean|beans]].{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Arai Hakuseki|Arai Hakuseki]]'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Yamagata Bantō|Yamakata Bantō]]'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]]'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|WD=Q11254|lt=salt}} or {{ill|Bean|en|Bean|WD=Q379813|lt=beans}}.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5623686]] Wikidata name: Gwisin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=There is an enwiki article but it seems horribly incomplete and possibly completely wrong scope[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 17:58, 13 February 2025 (UTC)|qq=}}
*
'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk beliefs}} of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|WD=Q11254|lt=salt}} or {{ill|Bean|en|Bean|WD=Q379813|lt=beans}}.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gwisin]][[es:Gwishin]][[fr:Gwishin]][[hu:Küsin]][[id:Gwishin]][[ja:鬼神]][[ko:귀신]][[zh:鬼神]]
*
'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk beliefs}} of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|WD=Q11254|lt=salt}} or {{ill|Bean|en|Bean|WD=Q379813|lt=beans}}.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk beliefs|1=Folk religion}} of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|WD=Q11254|lt=salt|1=Salt}} or {{ill|Bean|en|Bean|WD=Q379813|lt=beans|1=Bean}}.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki|1=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō|1=Yamagata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

*
'''Kishin''' (鬼神, {{lang-en|''Demonic Gods'' or ''Spiritual Deities''|qq=}}) refers to supernatural entities in various cultural traditions. When pronounced as "kijin" or "kishin," it primarily denotes spirits or deities of the heavens and earth. When read as "onigami," it refers to unseen spirits or fierce and terrifying gods.{{Sfn|Kotobank|2021|qq=}} == China == {{Chinese cultural elements | pinyin = guǐshén | katakana = グイシェン | hiragana = きしん|qq=}} In {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}, the term "鬼神" (guǐshén) refers to two contrasting types of spirits: "鬼" (guǐ, ghosts) and "神" (shén, gods). * '''Gui (鬼)''' – Malevolent spirits that bring misfortune to the living, manifesting as {{ill|evil ghosts|ja|悪鬼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evil ghosts|1=evil ghosts|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492603}}. They are often the subject of exorcisms and prayers.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Gui are the posthumous forms of the "魄" (pò), the yin-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the physical body. When a person dies, their pò remains in the earthly realm and transforms into a ghost. If they died a violent death or were left without a proper burial and veneration, they might become vengeful ghosts (幽鬼) and cause calamities.{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} * '''Shen (神)''' – Benevolent spirits that bring blessings to the living and are worshiped in {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} Shen originate from the "魂" (hún), the yang-energy spirit of a living person, which governs the mind and ascends to the heavens after death, becoming a deity or divine spirit.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Watanabe|2021|qq=}} Additionally, the term "鬼神" can refer to the mysterious forces beyond human comprehension that create, change, and destroy all things in the universe.{{Sfn|Makitani|2021|qq=}} == Korea == {{Korean cultural elements | hangeul = 귀신 | katakana = クィシン|qq=}} In the {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk beliefs|1=Folk religion}} of the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, "gwi-shin" (귀신) refers to spirits that, unlike ancestral or guardian spirits, do not have a designated host and exist as independent entities.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} This distinguishes them from spirits that reside within humans or natural elements, such as deities (신명, sinmyeong). However, over time, the distinction between these categories has blurred.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}}{{Sfn|Soul Worship|2021|qq=}} Gwi-shin are depicted as weak and ghostly beings, clad in fragile paper garments, with emaciated legs like glue-covered bones. They are powerless before divine beings and can shrink away when confronted by strong-willed humans.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Though typically invisible, they may be appeased with offerings of food. However, some gwi-shin are malevolent and bring misfortune.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} During the daytime, gwi-shin wander through the air aimlessly, but at night, they seek damp places to rest.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} They often reside in ancient trees, shadowy groves, underground spaces, or near wells and ponds. To keep them away, people scatter {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|WD=Q11254|lt=salt|1=Salt}} or {{ill|Bean|en|Bean|WD=Q379813|lt=beans|1=Bean}}.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Some gwi-shin have supernatural foresight and can predict calamities, warn of impending doom, or reveal the location of lost objects.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} However, they are generally depicted as being driven away by more powerful entities. In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki|1=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō|1=Yamagata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

In {{ill|Korean folklore|ja|朝鮮の伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, figures like {{ill|Cheoyong|ko|처용|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheoyong|qq=}}, who expelled evil spirits, appear on protective talismans (符籍) to ward off gwi-shin.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} Additionally, stronger gwi-shin may prey upon weaker ones.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} It is also believed that even divine spirits can turn into gwi-shin if they lose their place of worship or are forgotten by the living.{{Sfn|GWE|2021|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Global World Encyclopedia|publisher=Doosan Corporation|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%AC%BC%E7%A5%9E-50543#E7.B2.BE.E9.81.B8.E7.89.88.20.E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.9B.BD.E8.AA.9E.E5.A4.A7.E8.BE.9E.E5.85.B8 |title=鬼神 |publisher=Shogakukan |work=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |date=2021-07-16 |language=ja|qq=}} * {{Cite web|url=https://ko.wikisource.org/wiki/%EA%B8%80%EB%A1%9C%EB%B2%8C_%EC%84%B8%EA%B3%84_%EB%8C%80%EB%B0%B1%EA%B3%BC%EC%82%AC%EC%A0%84/%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90%C2%B7%EC%B2%A0%ED%95%99/%ED%95%9C%EA%B5%AD%EC%9D%98_%EC%A2%85%EA%B5%90/%EB%AF%BC_%EA%B0%84_%EC%8B%A0_%EC%95%99/%EC%88%AD_%EB%B0%B0#%EA%B7%80%EC%8B%A0 |title=귀신 (鬼神) |publisher=Global World Encyclopedia |date=2021-07-16 |language=ko|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Arai Hakuseki|en|Arai Hakuseki|WD=Q467616|lt=Arai Hakuseki|1=Arai Hakuseki}}'s {{ill|On Kishin|ja|鬼神論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/On Kishin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamagata Bantō|en|Yamagata Bantō|WD=Q6565600|lt=Yamakata Bantō|1=Yamagata Bantō}}'s {{ill|Yume no Shiro|ja|夢の代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yume no Shiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}}'s {{ill|Kishin Shinron|ja|鬼神新論|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishin Shinron|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
